data.title: A Gambling Portfolio In A Down Cycle Could Create Short-Term Gains
data.description:
Above: Despite a sector lag, a wild west economy rife with tariffs, rising credit card defaults, growing unemployment, and a sleepy Fed, there are portfolio strategies for the investor with cash sitting on the sidelines and low confidence in the current gambling sector.

  • These gambling stocks are holding up well against the tsunami of macro forces battering tourism, conventioneers and still moderating VIP action.
  • A portfolio that mixes stocks with domestic footprints, online gaming, and bargain-priced Asian leaders can produce a short-term upside strategy now.
  • We've selected six stocks we believe could offer round trip profits.
  • Absurd Vegas prices for rooms, food, beverages, and services are beginning to moderate. MGM's (MGM) Bill Hornbuckle at a BofA presentation recently agreed with customers that casino prices were ridiculously high.
  • He pledged MGM was going to cut prices. Convention bookings and VIP arrivals are holding their own. Asia is reaching toward 80% of pre-COVID GGR 2019 levels. There are sprouts for those who look hard enough.

The "Off the Sidelines" Gambling Portfolio

There's ample evidence that the entire gambling sector, brick and mortar and online, is undergoing a sustained lag. We and other contributors have advanced various theories on why this has happened. Across the board, negative outlooks on the stocks are rooted in a 6.7% drop in tourist arrivals to Vegas from late summer on, and conventions are down 8%. Air traffic is down 6%. Customers are taking a pass on Vegas and many other venues.

Our view is that spiraling prices of visits across the board to Vegas properties have reached a point of massive rejection from patrons. I have also pointed to bankroll fatigue as a reaction to inflationary pricing gone wild on the Strip. Tourists who still come are reducing gaming budgets and bargain hunting. The arrivals continue to slide, but are diminishing.

What's new that we found disturbing is that historically tourist complaints about high prices generally did not materially impact arrivals. But this time cancellations are rife throughout the customer tiers we checked. I also note the added burden of 10% higher wages imposed on the brick and mortar segment impacting EBITDA. Overall, it's not a pretty picture for investors. Yet a quiet appraisal of six key stocks in the sector has shown me a bullish path ahead worth considering for investors who have put much of their cash on the sidelines for now. It's not for everyone, but investors with a bit of a higher risk can craft a portfolio within a portfolio with four of these stocks at equal weight and two (MLCO and CZR) overweight with much higher short-term ceilings.

International baccarat play seems to be holding up the entire table game segment in Vegas. Canada, for a complexity of reasons, part of which may be related to tariffs, is down a hefty 33%, sending Vegas officials to that country to try and shore up positive outlooks with deals.

Yet despite all this negativity, not to mention the "Vegas is dying" prognostications we see emanating from many media sources, few draconian forecasters are digging very deep into the real reasons Vegas' irreplacability in the globe's leisure destinations will not only survive but prosper again.

In 2027, we expect the arrival of the new Hard Rock conversion from the Mirage on the Strip. My friend and former colleague Jim Allen heads HR. He's a tough-minded realist with high expectations for the Hard Rock brand in his native Atlantic City.

PENN Entertainment (PENN) is in the process of replacing personal service functions with fully digitized AI services. The theory here is that while certain on-property personal services need to remain, the overwhelming majority of things like reservations for shows, dining, check-in, and feedback on service can be handled digitally. PENN has made its share of blunders in online sports betting, but this one seems more firmly rooted in common sense. Our sources at PENN tell us the tests are going well.

Above: My discussions with old friends still active in the business largely agree with the above assessment, with one big difference. They all see an early 2026 upturn as price adjustments made now work through the system.

Finding a buy rationale in glum days for gambling stocks

So what we now have in the casino and online gaming sector is the invasion of macro issues that have taken a heavy toll on gross gaming revenue across the board. But amidst the fears and sliding numbers, certain stocks have appeared to absorb the negative headwinds and continue to function well. They're realistically valued and likely to move up if a slow but steady recovery in national GGR begins to show. If you're a solid believer that in the end casino gambling and its growing online partners can withstand the assault of negative headwinds, then consider this strategy. My portfolio below has these risks:

1. Black swan events. An unseen, unexpected spike in inflation that triggers a higher trend of increasing unemployment and subsequent decline in travel.

2. Investor loss of conviction on these stocks if we get two or three unexpected quarters forward with lackluster results that look like a longer recovery of share prices ahead.

3. Fed action with negative consequences for the entire market.

Our contrary view:

This approach is for investors who believe in the deeply rooted human proclivity to gamble, occasionally or regularly. You cannot conclude otherwise if you read books with wall paintings from ancient Greece and Rome showing people gambling. And such scenes are rife through history in all cultures. Gambling for leisure may rise or dip, but the practice has never disappeared, other than periodic government bans.

Macro economics clearly has won the current battle, but it's a battle, not a war. Personal friends still active in the executive ranks of brick and mortar casinos are worried for certain. But they believe 2026 will show the beginning of a significant recovery. Forward bookings for tour groups and conventioneers are moving ahead of forecasts.

The stocks I have selected for this special portfolio and the weightings reflect a deep dive into their long-term trading patterns from my personal archives accumulated over my 35 years as a senior executive in the sector.

My overall conviction is that at least a six-month hold on this portfolio could potentially generate strong returns.

Believing that entering a rabbit hole of masses of quant data and deep dives into valuations would be a mega challenge to readers, I have selected six stocks I believe are well positioned business-wise. They should be in your portfolio because of a) their proven resilience during this lull, and b) proven quality of the business and management.

Stock Price at writing Our PT 1Q26

Boyd Gaming Corp. (BYD) $78.74 $94.50

Rationale: Our long-sustaining conviction that BYD is by far a great allocator of capital, efficient, a company that runs strong properties in diverse geographies, and a solid balance sheet. Although earnings have lagged largely due to cost increases, I'm confident management will act to curtail the 7.1% increase in costs in 3Q25 reports.

Caesars Ent. (CZR) $19.78 $32.44

Rationale: This is a screaming bargain, in our view. There are issues, there always are, with CZR because of its sheer size. No operator comes close to its 65 million rewards members, property spread, and variations throughout the system of 55 casinos in the U.S., Canada, and Dubai. The stock's high visibility always plays both positive and negative with investors. But at its current price, it has fallen so low as to present one of the best buy on the dip stories in the entire sector.

Las Vegas Sands (LVS) $65.38 $70

Rationale: I have been a long-term fan of this stock. I have been one of the analysts who has constantly pondered why the immense power of LVS stock price has hardly moved over time. I have laid out many reasons I believe Mr. Market has stayed away, but none strong enough to move me off a Strong Buy call. I have stated many times before to clients and readers alike that when and if LVS was able to hold ground at $50 (when it traded in the high thirties), that would represent a breakthrough. It has happened. The stock has moved right up to the edge of my price target at $70. I'm not raising my PT yet but will wait until we have a final number on the total Macau GGR for the year.

Flutter Ent. (FanDuel) $198 $326

Rationale: Since it began trading on the NYSE last January, I have been bullish on the shares of FLUT because not only does it hold an edge in the huge US sports betting market, but also because of its internationally deployed online brands throughout the globe. I have had a $300-plus price target on the stock since it was traded on the NYSE. Its global reach and its leadership in the US, though relatively small compared to DraftKings, is still meaningful because competition is fierce and costly. Scale counts, and FLUT wins the day because it's global vs. DKNG, which is not.

Light & Wonder (LNW) $87.39 $98.34

Rationale: Diversity of products in gaming machinery, persistently strong margins, and ability to increase market share against giants like ITT. Nearly fully valued, but I like the spark of tech savvy that usually results in quick adaptation by customers. I see activity in replacement equipment rising during 2026. A flood tide of new apps is on the way, drenched in AI.

Melco Resorts (MLCO) $8.90 $16.

Rationale: This fallen star of the early 2000s in the Asia sector was and is the creation of the aggressive CEO Lawrence Ho of the notable Ho gaming family. Its most recent reports show invigorated Asian revenue and profitability. Gains in the Macau and Philippines markets by MLCO despite macro bearish trends that are just beginning to turn positive in Asia. Its new Cyprus property has shown growth. MLCO has now returned among the Asian gaming stocks to watch because it's outperforming its stock price.

Summary: My thesis here is this: If you don't own any of the six noted above, they are a buy. If you do hold all or some, don't sell yet, but think of the premise I also raise. My view is that all will be higher in 2026.

I have taken into account some bearish signs appearing in all of these stocks that do not support my bullish assessment. But weighing the asset values and the management track records, I see value that Mr. Market has yet to price into the trades. That's where my conviction originates from. Begin now, hold until 2Q 2026, take profits, and move on these stocks as they then fit into your general portfolio.

data.title: Japan Breaks The Markets: Can The Fed Fix Them?
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Markets have seen their worst losing streak since the April sell-off, and investors are looking for the culprit.

High valuations, volatility in the repo market, and even political instability domestically could be cited as possible culprits.

But, once again, I fear the real catalyst might be what’s happening in Japan, a country that for years has served as the source of cheap money for the world.

Japan is now undergoing some significant challenges, with a new government wanting to stimulate, a Central Bank ready to hike, a Yen that keeps sliding, and yields that keep climbing.

All of this translates into a lot of uncertainty and potential headwinds for liquidity.

However, even if Japan takes a step back from being the world’s ATM, I think the Federal Reserve will soon step in to provide the market with what it needs.

Japan’s Catch-22

Japan has always led the way in terms of monetary and even fiscal policy, and it may be showing what the endgame looks like. After years of ZIRP, QE and a record debt-to-GDP, things are beginning to get shaky for the nation.

For some time, the BoJ kept yields close to zero, even though this led to a structurally weak yen. This was acceptable in an era of deflation, but now things have changed.

Now that inflation has crept in, the story has changed. A weak yen has imported inflation at an accelerating pace, and that’s something that the BoJ has to be wary of now.

The BoJ is, in fact, ready to tighten in order to curb inflation and support the Yen, but that’s also pushing Japanese 10-year yields to record levels.

This is at a time when the new Central government is looking to stimulate the economy to the tune of 2 trillion yen.

This friction even prompted a meeting between the BoJ Governor and the current PM earlier this week.

Printing is inflationary, while tightening could put further strain on the bond market. There is no safe option.

Markets Don’t Like This

Due to this dynamic, the world has benefited from the Japanese yen carry trade. Cheap Japanese funding, which flowed into foreign assets, especially US equities.

The BoJ printed, the yen weakened, and global risk assets benefited from a steady drip-feed of Japanese liquidity.

Now we are once again getting a taste of this reversing.

Back in August of 2024, we saw what happens if the Yen appreciates too fast. This unwinds the carry trade.

Now, we’re seeing this unwind in the other direction.

The spread between US Treasuries and Japanese 10-year bonds is plummeting, which also unwinds the carry trade.

Suddenly, Japanese assets are more attractive, and money begins to flow back to Japan instead of to the US and foreign markets.

I Believe the US Will Step In

What we are seeing in Japan could be a permanent shift.

The three decades of easy money from Japan may end, but that doesn’t mean risk assets or US equities are suddenly uninvestable.

For starters, this is only one piece in a very complex macro puzzle.

But, more importantly, even if Japan leaves a liquidity hole, so to speak, I believe the US will step in to fill it.

This is a case I have made for some time, and I believe it’s a policy that the US and the Federal Reserve will actively pursue.

In order to help reduce the debt burden while keeping tax receipts elevated, a combination of higher inflation and continued asset price increases is needed.

This will happen through a variety of policies.

  • Earlier Fed rate cuts

  • A faster return to QE

  • Liquidity injections via reserve management

  • Expansion of swap lines to stabilize dollar funding

  • Aggressive fiscal spending

This is especially true as we head into midterm elections, with strong incentives for the current administration to make things look as good as possible.

This is why I see the current sell-off as a temporary dislocation, not the start of a structural downturn. Japan’s pain becomes America’s justification for easing.

Final Thoughts

Japan’s sudden tightening has delivered the first major macro shock of 2025. The BoJ is trapped in an impossible situation: tighten and break the system, or loosen and break the currency. There is no painless path forward, and markets have reacted accordingly.

But investors need to look beyond the panic.

Japan’s crisis is not the end of the global cycle; it is the beginning of the next policy pivot. The BoJ’s actions will drain liquidity, tighten funding conditions, and destabilize risk assets in the short term.

For investors, the next few months may remain volatile, but this environment ultimately sets the stage for renewed upside in 2026. What feels like the start of a crisis today may be the prelude to the next liquidity cycle tomorrow, and I believe it will be an aggressive one.

data.title: Oshkosh Corporation: This Bumpy Ride Will End In A Great Spot
data.description:
Even though I've established a great track record for myself, not all of my calls turn out to be good ones. An example of one opportunity that has significantly lagged is Oshkosh Corporation (OSK), a $7.62 billion niche vehicle and vehicle parts producer here in the United States. Back in July of 2021, I called it an attractive opportunity. The company had a good operating history, and shares looked, at worst, fairly valued. But since then, things have gone a bit south.

To be clear, actual revenue, profits, and cash flows grew nicely over the last three completed fiscal years. But this year, we have seen some weakness. As a result, shares are up only 12.9% since my last article about the business, which pales in comparison to the 56.9% increase that the S&P 500 has enjoyed. As disappointing as this is to see, I don't think investors should despair. Shares of the company look incredibly cheap on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar enterprises. And even though I fully expect some near-term pressures, I am bullish enough to maintain a very solid "Buy" rating.

Taking Oshkosh for a test drive

Given how much time has gone on since my last article about Oshkosh, I think it would be helpful to dig briefly into the firm's operations. As I mentioned already, it plays in the vehicle and vehicle parts space. However, management would more accurately describe it as a global industrial technology company. It does this through the production and sale of certain vehicles and equipment, including those used for things like firefighting, refuse and recycling collection, construction, the aviation industry, the defense sector, and more.

There really are three separate operating segments for the business. The first and largest of these is its Access segment, which produces and sells access and material handling equipment used in the construction sector, the agricultural industry, the vegetation management space, and elsewhere. Major companies that purchased from it include equipment rental firms, construction contractors, home improvement centers, and the list goes on.

The Vocational segment is a bit smaller but still very sizable. This enterprise produces commercial and custom fire apparatus, airport ground support equipment and gate equipment, and it even provides baggage and facility airport services for customers. There are other examples of what this segment does as well, but you get the idea. And then finally, the Transport segment produces and sells specialty vehicles and mobility systems used by the U.S. Department of Defense. Examples here include heavy tactical vehicles, medium tactical vehicles, medium-caliber weapons systems, and joint light tactical vehicles.

Over the last few years, the financial performance for the company had been largely positive. From 2022 through 2024, revenue jumped from $8.28 billion to $10.73 billion. As sales improved, profitability got better as well. Net income skyrocketed from $173.9 million to $681.4 million during this time. Adjusted net profits even grew nicely, shooting up from $237.6 million to $772.7 million. As the chart above illustrates, most cash flow metrics for the business increased during this time as well.

As great as this is to see, 2025 has proven to be a difficult time for the business. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first nine months of 2024 compared to the same time this year. Revenue fell rather meaningfully from $8.13 billion to $7.73 billion. Naturally, profitability took a hit as well. Net income contracted from $528.3 million to $513.2 million. Adjusted net profits dropped from $603.4 million to $552 million. Although operating cash flow improved, both adjusted operating cash flow and EBITDA for the business worsened.

I think that an insightful discussion of the drivers behind this decline is in order. But before I get into the bad news, I would like to touch on the good news. The fact of the matter is that one part of the company that has been consistently positive is the Vocational segment. After seeing revenue jump from $2.18 billion in 2022 to $3.31 billion last year, this year, the $2.80 billion generated for the first nine months comfortably exceeded the $2.43 billion reported a year earlier. This surge in revenue was driven by two primary things. Mostly, it was thanks to higher sales volumes that benefited the top line to the tune of $235 million. In addition to this, the firm saw a $140 million benefit from higher pricing. Seeing volumes increase as prices go up is always a great thing. That means that market conditions are strong.

Management mentioned that, for some time, there had been production bottlenecks that negatively affected the business. But by undertaking multiyear initiatives, they have been working to boost sales here, including when it comes to the fire trucks that it produces. By working on the operational side here and also by making major investments such as those involving intelligent and connected products, electrification opportunities, autonomy, and more, the business expects to continue growing this segment. Over the last five years, the company has also boosted its contact with potential customers, as evidenced by the fact that its Pierce dealer service locations have jumped by 30% over the last five years. When you add all of this together, and you consider that the backlog for this segment has jumped from $3.5 billion to $6.3 billion over the last three completed fiscal years, it's not difficult to imagine management hitting their target of between $4.6 billion and $5 billion by the year 2028 compared to the $3.31 billion generated this year. And naturally, as revenue here increases, so too does profitability.

Unfortunately, this is where the good news ends. After seeing revenue rise from $3.97 billion in 2022 to $5.16 billion last year, the Access segment has taken a hit. The $3.32 billion generated by the unit in the first nine months of this year fell short of the $4.01 billion reported for the previous year. Management blamed this on lower organic sales volumes of $698 million that ended up being caused by what they described as softer market conditions and the expiration of the agreement to produce Caterpillar (CAT) branded telehandlers. Meanwhile, the Transport segment for the business reported a rise in revenue over the last three years, only to see sales drop from $1.62 billion in the first nine months of 2024 to $1.53 billion at the same time this year. This drop was caused by lower sales volumes for one of its programs with the U.S. Department of Defense. But honestly, this is not terribly surprising. Defense programs typically do see volatility, with some projects ramping up while others scale down as they near the end of their lives. Regardless, even though revenue contracted here, at least profitability improved, which is the opposite of what we saw with the Access segment.

Despite these troubles, the future for the company looks bright. Yes, management expects revenue this year to drop to between $10.3 billion and $10.4 billion compared to the $10.73 billion reported for last year. Adjusted earnings per share are forecasted to be between $10.50 and $11, which would mean adjusted net profits at the midpoint of $694.4 million. This is a negative compared to the $772.7 million the company reported for last year. And if we project out results based on what we have seen already for the year, we would anticipate adjusted operating cash flow of $700 million and EBITDA of $1.16 billion.

If all goes according to plan, however, management expects growth to return soon. In fact, the company anticipates that revenue in 2028 will be between $13 billion and $14 billion. This should result in adjusted earnings per share of between $18 and $22, which at the midpoint would be about $1.29 billion for adjusted net profits. Scaling up other profitability metrics similarly would give us adjusted operating cash flow of $1.75 billion and EBITDA of $2.16 billion.

Using these estimates, the estimates for 2025, and the historical results for 2024, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently valued. In addition to these multiples being low on an absolute basis, they are also low compared to some other similar firms. In the table below, I stacked Oshkosh up against five such businesses. What I found was that, on both a price-to-earnings basis and on a price-to-operating-cash-flow basis, only one of the five firms was cheaper than our candidate. And when it came to the EV-to-EBITDA approach, Oshkosh was in fact the cheapest of the group.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Oshkosh Corporation 11.0 8.1 7.4
Federal Signal Corporation (FSS) 29.5 28.1 17.4
Allison Transmission (ALSN) 9.9 8.7 7.7
Atmus Filtration Technologies (ATMU) 20.4 23.5 14.7
Terex Corporation (TEX) 19.9 7.6 9.4
REV Group (REVG) 25.2 11.7 13.5

One thing that I think is interesting is if we assume that management will achieve its targets for 2028. If it does, and if it continues to trade at the same multiples that it's trading at today, we can see in the chart below what the annualized upside for shareholders would be based on the trading multiples we looked at already. Here, we'd be looking at an upside of between 22.1% and 24.1%. Again, that's annualized. That's well above what the broader market enjoys. And it deserves some attention.

Takeaway

Currently, I am very bullish about Oshkosh. As time goes on, I think that the rest of the market will come to realize that it should be as well. Normally, with figures like this, I would even go so far as to rate the enterprise a "Strong Buy." But because I am worried about the economy more broadly, I wouldn't be surprised if there is some near-term pain. And for that reason, I think that rating the company a very solid "Buy" makes sense.

data.title: BIZD: Private Credit Takes On Major Challenges
data.description:
Today, we leave the world of commercial real estate and venture towards another segment of high yield. As one can imagine, REITer's Digest typically sticks around the world of real estate, exploring the good, the bad, and the ugly of brick and mortar. Outside of my publications on real estate, I venture into other parts of the high-yield universe where cash flow and income take precedence over speculation and innovation.

Over the years, business development companies and the world of private credit have become an increasing portion of my coverage. Over the past 15 years, we have seen the proliferation of private lending from money managers who moved to fill the void of lending left after the Great Financial Crisis. As traditional banks began to tighten their underwriting standards, funds moved to fill the gap and become a primary source of capital for private equity-sponsored companies looking to recapitalize or expand. In other words, funds moved to capitalize on opportunities deemed too risky by traditional banks.

This trend led to the proliferation of business development companies, publicly traded vehicles that loaned to private companies with sub-investment-grade credit ratings. The largest representative exchange-traded fund in the segment is the VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD), the topic of today's article.

In this discussion, we will dive deeper into recent developments in the private credit sphere, explain the implications for business development companies, and assess whether these are cracks in the systems or an isolated instance. First, let's follow up on my prior coverage of the fund.

Review of Prior Coverage

BIZD is my fund of choice when covering the business development company sector. While there are other participants, such as an actively managed fund from Putnam BDC Income ETF (PBDC), this one takes the cake as the largest and most liquid opportunity to target the segment as a whole. The fund is weighted by market capitalization, meaning it is heavily weighted towards some of the largest business development companies, including Ares Capital (ARCC). In fact, ARCC accounts for a large portion of the fund.

Over the past year, I have covered the fund on several occasions. Generally, my coverage is either discussing market dynamics that could be impacting the sector as a whole or assessing whether passive investing is really the best approach for business development companies. The topic of today's article will dive deeper into the first category as we assess whether recent major bankruptcies present systemic risk to business development companies.

I published an article earlier this year when I called business development companies a sector Buy by following turmoil from tariffs. Despite the reality that many clients of business development companies stand directly in the crosshairs of tariffs, the pricing of funds like BIZD as well as the ongoing rate cuts. Despite the negative impact of falling interest rates, combined with a questionable macroeconomic picture, I believe business development companies are still well managed, well diversified, and generate ample cash flow to be attractive for a high-yield investment.

Since the publication of my prior article, BIZD has struggled on the heels of two major bankruptcies. It has underperformed some of the sector’s largest due to issues facing certain positions like FS KKR Capital Corp (FSK) and Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN). These two underperformers have been facing portfolio weakness and valuation issues, respectively, over the past six months.

For an index fund like BIZD, this means that the fund will feel a certain degree of the sector's pain in an unavoidable way.

Let's shift the perspective and begin looking ahead. There are meaningful developments in the business development company sector and private lending sphere which could be arbiters of coming trouble.

Trouble In Paradise

Warren Buffett has been one of the generational talents in investing. When the Oracle of Omaha speaks, others listen. Over the years, Buffett has been relatively silent on public credit, sticking closely to his guns in the world with Berkshire Hathaway. In the few instances where he has opined on the health and safety of this relatively young industry, he has been relatively muted with his commentary.

To summarize Buffett’s long-term commentary, it’s simply too soon to tell. The industry was too green to truly show its durability and resilience, having never faced a chaotic era. Even recently, increased bankruptcies caused by macro weakness and rising interest rates have been muted. Private credit has powered through, still not facing any sort of systemic crisis comparable to the Great Financial Crisis.

This means that the trillions of dollars of capital that continue to pile into private credit are untested. The money managers who received the overwhelming amount of this capital have not been through a truly tumultuous era. This means that the variance of experience at the fund level is wide. The implications remain unknown as the industry continues to power ahead, but those who can see trouble coming before it arrives are generally rewarded.

Recently, the high-profile bankruptcies of two companies in the private credit sphere have taken center stage in the conversation around business development companies and lending to private companies.

...it’s inevitable that greater risk will enter the system in the form of more aggressive and sometimes unscrupulous operators. As the cycle progresses, more questionable credits tend to multiply, especially if the economy softens and credit conditions tighten. At such times, we believe thorough research and analysis of issuer fundamentals has the potential to help surface potential issues. First Brands’s aggressive approach, lack of oversight and closely held operation were all potential indicators, as were Tricolor’s integration of financing and its stellar recovery rates, along with its narrow and economically vulnerable business.

The bankruptcies of First Brands and Tricolor have sent shockwaves through securitized lending and the sub-investment-grade credit world. With the quantities of CLOs that had exposure to First Brands, investors and money managers alike are beginning to question the significance of just one bankruptcy. BIZD has begun to experience the pain and consequences of such a significant bankruptcy. Investor psychology is coming into play.

A Matter of Perspective

The reality of credit investing is that things move in slow motion. Our perception of reality moves much faster than reality itself causing our perceptions to go from very high to very low very quickly. In the case of a major bankruptcy like First Brands and Tricolor, investors are quick to flee viewing them as a cockroach in the kitchen. The idea that these two bankruptcies could be the first of many has begun to increase investor scrutiny towards the private lending and private credit worlds.

In reality, the impact of these two bankruptcies is much less significant than our minds are making us believe. Keep in mind that the loans issued by these companies receive enormous spreads because of the sub-investment-grade credit profile of the borrower. Implied in these credit spreads is that a single-digit portion of these companies will go bankrupt each year. The question on the part of the manager is whether the spreads they lend at are adequate to compensate the shareholder for these anticipated credit risks. This is the very nature of credit investing and highlights the importance of lenders to make judicious decisions during the best of times.

The decisions you make during the best of times will be the situations that you are forced to handle during the worst of times.

Private credit is tougher than public credit, as investors have much less information to root their decisions. This leaves credit spreads to compensate for the elevated risk profile of lending to these unqualified borrowers who are unable to approach a larger financial institution.

However, this approach could be fundamentally incorrect. As I mentioned above, in order to be right in credit, the investor needs to be correct before everybody else. Once the risks begin to be recognized and digested, the idea that an investor will be able to capitalize on an unseen opportunity quickly diminishes. In the case of business development companies and their corresponding funds, the bankruptcy of large borrowers can come as a shock to the system, forcing a quick reassessment of credit risks. For a forward-looking investor who understands that the actual system itself is unchanged and that these realities happen as part of investing in the sub-investment-grade world, the long-term appeal of a fund like BIZD increases at a discounted price. With other opportunities such as an actively managed fund, PBDC, there are better opportunities to invest in BDCs. With that in mind, BIZD earns a Hold rating as business development companies continue to digest recent changes.

data.title: Adobe: The Semrush Acquisition Strengthens Its AI Chops
data.description:
Amid the stock market rally this year, it's mainly large caps that have driven the S&P 500's (SP500) gains while small and mid-cap stocks have floundered. Amid the crash in small-cap multiples to eye-watering lows, dealmaking activity is picking up in both private equity and corporate acquirers, as evidenced by Adobe Inc.'s (ADBE) recent $1.9 billion cash acquisition of SEO specialist Semrush Holdings, Inc. (SEMR).

Since the start of the year, Adobe shares have fallen nearly 30%, though that decline wasn't as steep as Semrush's, which had fallen about ~40% before the deal was announced at a ~70% premium to its pre-announcement share price.

Previously, I had written a Buy rating on Adobe in August and was long on Semrush as well. In my view, the deal strengthens the AI portfolio for Adobe, and despite the eye-watering premium, Adobe is still paying a modest price for a business with recurring revenue and healthy cash flow. I am reiterating my Buy opinion for Adobe, as the creative software giant looks to use its expansive balance sheet and healthy cash flow to bolster its growth via organic and inorganic means.

Acquisition at a modest <4x revenue multiple

Adobe shares fell ~2% immediately following news of the transaction, but in my view, the company is paying a more-than-reasonable price to acquire Semrush.

For FY25, Semrush had recently guided to $443.5-$444.5 million in revenue, or 18% y/y growth, alongside 12% FCF margins (indicating $53.3 million of FCF).

Adobe's price tag for Semrush is $1.9 billion, but assuming Adobe takes control of the $275.7 million of cash on Semrush's latest balance sheet, the actual enterprise value of the deal is $1.62 billion. This means that Adobe is buying Semrush for 3.6x EV/FY25 revenue and ~30x FCF.

These aren't outlandish premiums, despite Semrush's ~70% rally on the news. There are a few enterprise software companies that are growing at a mid-teens clip and are trading at <4x revenue (larger-cap companies, such as Workday, Inc. (WDAY).

We note as well that Adobe had $5.9 billion of debt-free cash on its most recent balance sheet, so this acquisition is relatively small for the company.

In fact, Adobe also generated $2.2 billion of operating cash flow and $2.0 billion of free cash flow in its most recent quarter. In effect, Adobe could take on an acquisition of Semrush's size every quarter with ongoing cash flow alone.

Competitive synergies

In my view, the Adobe/Semrush deal brings together plenty of sales synergies that, in particular, can help Semrush unlock a wider audience for its SEO tools, particularly as the latter focuses on how to help companies with brand discovery in a time when AI chatbots are rising in prominence.

Recall that one of Semrush's biggest issues with sales this year is a rise in churn in the company's smaller, less sophisticated customer segment. Back in its Q2 earnings call, CEO Bill Wagner noted as follows:

While we are excited by the growth and adoption of our enterprise and AI products this quarter, we continue to experience softness at the lower end of the market. This customer segment includes freelancers and less sophisticated users who have historically had the highest churn rate of our customer cohorts.

We also saw a dramatic increase in paid search cost per click in the quarter, which increased our cost to acquire these customers. In the face of the declining unit economics for this lower-value customer segment and given the strong returns we are seeing from our investments in AI search and the enterprise segment, we made a strategic decision to not increase our marketing spend, which would have pursued volume and near-term revenue at the expense of long-term value."

Semrush has responded by trying to move to upmarket clients (which to some degree is working; in Q3, Semrush reported a 72% y/y increase in customers with greater than >$50,000 in ARR.

This is where Adobe can slot in quite nicely. Adobe already has a large base of consumer customers for its Creative Cloud products. One of Semrush's issues is that it lacked the marketing budget and the sales team to reach out to smaller customers, but Adobe can package Semrush tools within its own portfolio as well as cross-sell to existing customers.

And while Semrush is small in terms of Adobe's scale, it does provide a much-needed growth kick for Adobe, which the markets have long viewed as a net loser in the AI arms race, especially as more modern tools like Figma, Inc. (FIG), which went public this year, encroach into Adobe's territory with advanced design tools and automated slide creation engines.

In Q3, Adobe grew its Digital Media ARR at 11.7% y/y to $18.59 billion, with its growth rate decelerating gradually over the past several quarters. Semrush, meanwhile, comes with $455.4 million of ARR (at a faster 14% y/y growth rate), which will immediately provide a ~2.5% boost to Adobe's ARR when the deal closes (expected to close in the first half of 2026). We like the fact that Semrush will also be accretive to Adobe's profitability and cash flow from Day 1.

In my view, Semrush's product also fits in nicely with Adobe's AI strategy. The most prominent of Adobe's AI products is Adobe Firefly, which is a creative AI suite that lets paid subscribers generate and edit video, images, and audio. What Adobe is lacking, however, are tools that are more geared toward its enterprise software business, and Semrush, which recently pivoted to AI SEO optimization, offers exactly this. Furthermore, Semrush's nascent AI products are growing at a rapid clip, with CEO Bill Wagner remarking on the Q3 earnings call that AI ARR has sequentially doubled from Q2:

Our AI search products, including our AI Toolkit and AI Optimization products that were launched this year, added $10 million in ARR in the quarter, more than doubling from Q2 to Q3. These products are not only attracting new customers to Semrush, but we're also seeing unprecedented growth from our existing customers, where our average ARR per customer has increased 17% year-over-year."

All in all, Adobe will benefit from a fuller enterprise AI product suite, while Semrush can take advantage of Adobe's cross-sell capacity to cover lost ground at the lower end of the market, where Semrush's cost of customer acquisition has grown too high for the company to pursue new leads on its own.

Key takeaways

Investors who had patiently held Semrush up to this point can benefit from the knowledge here that decimated value stocks will typically have eager buyers window-shopping for M&A deals. We shouldn't ignore the appeal in Adobe either after a sharp YTD slide. Wall Street is currently expecting $23.33 in pro forma EPS for the company in FY26 (+12% y/y, and before the consolidation of Semrush), indicating that Adobe trades at just 13.6x FY26 P/E (or just 13.0x on an ex-cash basis, excluding the ~$6 billion of net cash on Adobe's books). This is a bargain not just against the broader S&P 500, but amid software peers as well.

Take advantage of this dislocation between Adobe's opportunity and its ultralow valuation to buy the stock.

data.title: ASTX: 2x ETF On ASTS Is Like Rocket Fuel For The Daring
data.description:
Introduction To The Tradr 2X Long ASTS Daily ETF

The Tradr 2X Long ASTS Daily ETF (ASTX) follows in the tradition of dozens of other leveraged single stock ETFs:

  • It is based on a stock AST SpaceMobile (ASTS) that at the time of the ETF launch, exhibited extremely high volatility. How high? Oh, "only" about 6x that of the S&P 500 ETF (SPY) during its first few years as a public company. That excludes the times ASTS suffered steep price drops, which naturally carried over to ASTX, bursting the volatility bubble that for stocks in their infancy (which as of this writing, is the case) is never-ending.

This is an informational article, and focuses on ASTX, rather than ASTS. However, ASTS is the underlying stock here, and so any decision one makes to own ASTX is essentially coupled with a pre-requisite regarding ASTS.

Specifically, one does not have to love the company. They just have to like the stock, temporarily. To that end, leveraged ETFs on single stocks like this one are a consideration for a range of investors beyond those who track the company's business closely.

Because while some investors are focused on fundamentals, others simply want to "catch a flyer," perhaps as more of a garnish alongside their main dish: a portfolio of stocks, ETFs, bonds, etc. That leads to this conclusion:

2 reasons to consider owning ASTX

  1. You believe in the company and want to "crush it" on the upside, having no fear of the downside. Because the swings are wide enough due to leverage. But as a stock goes through the process of maturing as a business and as a public company, there are many reasons its stock price can and likely will be tossed around. A lot more than a blue chip equity. As we'll see below, there is a timing element here, regardless of how optimistic one is on the stock longer term.
  2. You want to, as I say, "take big shots with small amounts of money," viewing this and other leveraged ETFs as akin to buying call options on stocks. But without an expiration date. That said, ASTX as much as any leveraged single stock ETF is at risk of going to zero. I'll explain why shortly.

What is the ASTX ETF?

ASTX was launched on July 10, 2025. The fund has accumulated $107M in Assets Under Management and it carries an expense ratio of 1.3%. The fund is pretty actively traded for a newer fund with ~937,000 shares changing hands on average each day.

From the ETF's own communication material:

  • Tradr 2X Long ASTS Daily ETF is an exchange traded fund launched by Investment Managers Series Trust II.
  • The fund is managed by AXS Investments LLC. The fund invests through derivatives in stocks of companies operating across information technology, technology hardware and equipment, communications equipment, telephone and telecommunications equipment sectors. The fund uses derivatives such as swaps and options to create its portfolio.
  • The investment seeks daily investment results, before fees and expenses, that correspond to two times (200%) the daily performance of the common shares of AST SpaceMobile, Inc.

So, this ETF is a pure play, not a diversified portfolio. That alone carries high risk of loss, temporary and beyond. A quick glance at the 52-week range in ASTS, the underlying stock that ASTX is based on, indicates just how wild the volatility is here. $102.79 to $17.50 in a year's time.

As of this writing, the company had just announced quarterly revenue of less than $15mm. And earnings are some number of years away.

Here is a chart of ASTX's first few months of trading, alongside that of ASTS. The leveraged effect can produce dramatic results when the stock goes up and up. And on the way down, the opposite, and the math of loss works against the investor.

What's that? The idea that even though ASTX is reset each day in regards to its 2x leverage to ASTS, if the ETF loses a percentage amount when you hold it, then loses more, you need more than the sum of those losses to get back to even.

At a glance, ASTS's business, if it is able to survive long-term and thrive, can potentially produce significant long-term returns to shareholders.

However, its fate may depend in part on the continued ability to borrow funds to grow. So rising or elevated interest rates, as well as how the market rewards earlier-stage companies, might be more important to determining its stock price over time, rather than traditional fundamentals. This is a risk until the company reaches the size and stability in its business to make it otherwise.

Investors can own ASTS, or they can own ASTX. Or neither. But since ASTS offers options trading, that creates a third, "off the radar" approach that could potentially allow one to take their shot in space so to speak, while capping their downside risk. That's something the ETF and stock do not do on their own. Here's a quick sample, for format, not recommendation.

How an option collar on this (or any other) stock might work, incorporating a 2x leveraged ETF

This is one of many possible scenarios. This option collar goes out more than two years from the time this article was written. In addition to owning 100 shares of ASTS, a protective put at a $55 strike price and a covered call at an $80 strike price cost nearly the same. So this is a low cost (not quite a zero-cost) collar.

Less the 3% cost, that $80 to $55 price range for ASTS over a 25-month time frame is set. Should the stock be called away at $80, the net return is more than 34.6%. If it were to fall below $50 and stay there, the holder of the collar could sell it for no more than 8.4% downside risk. So that's about a 4:1 upside to downside tradeoff.

Note that this type of structure requires good option liquidity. But furthermore, this example is more about showing how investors can potentially get a foothold into the underlying stock with boundaries. That produces a potential role for ASTX.

That role? The call option "surrogate." If ASTS were to reach that $80 cap level from the call option, that's a nice gain. However, with a stock like this, $80 could just be a stop along the way. So whether tactically or otherwise, the availability of ASTX allows the collar to stay in place, but provides a convenient way for a bullish ASTS shareholder to participate in growth of that stock above the $80 level. Naturally, ASTX can be used along the way to accentuate the existing, assumed 100 share position in ASTS.

This is a somewhat exotic strategy, but one that investors put into play every day. Most importantly, it is not specific to this stock. Many stocks have leveraged ETFs, inverse ETFs and even leveraged-inverse ETFs.

While ASTX is without a doubt a speculative vehicle, if investors interested in it manage their position size prudently, they can exert some risk management over an otherwise wild and crazy ETF, ASTX.

This article answers these three main questions about ASTX:

  • Should ASTX be considered a high risk/high reward fund?
  • Is ASTX better suited for short-term trading or long-term investing?
  • What type of investor is ASTX best suited for?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for education purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Commodity Catalysts: How AI Demand And Energy Transition Could Propel Commodities Higher
data.description:
As demand from artificial intelligence grows so does the appetite for metals and minerals essential to its growth. Tim Yang, VP for Commodities at TD Asset Management, joins MoneyTalk to discuss how AI is reshaping the commodity space and what it means for production, pricing and markets.

Transcript

Anthony Okolie: This year we've seen record runs for commodities like gold and copper. But what could be the possible catalyst to push the space higher in 2026? Joining us now to discuss is Tim Yang, Vice President for Commodities with TD Asset Management.

And Tim, welcome to the show again.

Tim Yang: Thank you for having me.

Anthony Okolie: Alright. So we're nearing the end of 2025. Can you recap us what we've seen in commodity markets this year?

Tim Yang: For sure. 2025-- what a year. Lots of volatility, lots of uncertainties. A lot of things happened this year. So, if I can pick one commodity to represent 2025, that would be gold.

Gold has a record year. This year gold price appreciated by 50%. Usually, gold prices don't move that much-- but when it moves, it moves a lot. It's telling a signal-- something's happening. The world is changing. So what has changed?

The first thing I want to mention is the tariff, tariff announcement in April. Liberation Day tariff announcement from the United States is a shock to the world that the existing trade policy doesn't work anymore. We need to re-adjust. So countries have been rushing to make deals with the United States. Businesses have been holding off the investment. They are waiting for what happens to tariff impact. So that's the first reason to buy gold, because gold is a perfect hedge for uncertainties.

The second reason to buy gold is stored value. So what happened in September-- US central bank Fed have been cutting rates in September and October, twice, despite the inflation is still above the target. So, in the dual mandate of Fed-- fighting inflation versus maximum employment-- Fed has chosen the employment as the priorities, then the market thinks the inflation risk is going to be higher. They buy gold for hedging inflation.

So both functions of gold-- hedge uncertainty and hedge inflation-- has been utilized this year. And that's the main reason we think gold has a record year.

Anthony, 2025 is a special year. 10 years from now, if you're looking back, this year, it's got to be one of the defining moments in history books.

Anthony Okolie: Yeah, it's been a remarkable year in 2025. Given that backdrop, what are some of the key themes that you'll be watching going forward?

Tim Yang: Sure. If 2025 is a shock, 2026 is "get used to it". This is the new environment.

Anthony Okolie: This is the norm.

Tim Yang: This is the norm. Get used to this. We might see higher tariff. We might see higher inflation risk. This is the environment we are operating. We're not making any judgment whether the decision-making from the government or central banks were right or not. This is the reality.

Think about this. It's been seven months since April. Countries have been making deals with the United States. Very few countries have retaliated against US. So the world has realized, this is the new game.

Under this framework, and if we have higher tariffs, high inflation, there's a lot of investment decisions [that] can be made. The policy uncertainty in 2026 and going forward is likely to be reduced.

The other thing I want to mention is, we are going to likely operate in a loose monetary and fiscal policy environment. Market has been pricing a few rate cuts in the United States, and that's likely to be bearish on the dollar. We also see fiscal spending in a lot of places-- in Europe, the US, in Canada-- so those are going to be very positive for commodities. So there are a lot of themes we can play under those consumptions.

There is artificial intelligence and data center buildout. There is energy infrastructures. There's energy transition themes. And also, there is a US and China competition. A lot of them has impact on commodity demand.

Anthony Okolie: OK. Let's delve a little bit more into that, how AI has impacted commodity demand. What are you expecting there going forward?

Tim Yang: Yeah. So artificial intelligence is topical, and the impact on commodity demand is massive.

So first, you need to build a data center. Literally, you need to build a building. A lot of materials [are] needed to be in the buildings. You need to have supercomputers. So copper, alloy, they all need to be in this. This is number one.

Number two, you need to generate power-- because data center, the power draw was massive, is 24/7.

And the third thing is the infrastructures. Right now in a lot of places, like in Canada, in the US, the infrastructure is very old. It's aging infrastructure. It's been 100 years old. We need massive investment to upgrade and modernize the grid so they can serve power to data centers and, in addition, to regular business and consumers.

Each data center-- hyperscale data center-- is like [the] equivalent of a medium-sized city. The power need is the same as-- and serving hundreds of thousands of homes. So there is a massive need to address that issue.

Anthony Okolie: Alright. Let's talk about the energy transition, which may have been lost a little bit in the AI theme. What impact will that have on commodities going forward that you see, at least?

Tim Yang: Yeah. So, the energy transition is very topical as well. In a lot of countries in the world, decarbonization, it is the priority.

One example is the EV sales. The EV sales this year, year to date, the world sales is 16.5 million EVs. That's 23% year-over-year growth. So this trend is still going. I want to buy EV, too-- it's a great thing to have. And then each EV, compared to ICE vehicle, contains six times as much minerals than the traditional ICE cars. And also the EV weighs heavier, so it's a lot more metals needed to go to EV.

The other thing I want to mention is, for countries around the world that don't have oil and gas, energy security is another reason for them to build renewable energies. We have seen in 2022 the energy shock, commodity shock-- for countries that need to import energy, [that] is a problem.

So, there was a massive demand for countries to move away from a fossil-fueled energy base into a more diversified, sustainable, reliable energy source. That becomes a national strategic priority for all of them. So the demand for commodities in [the] energy space will be here to stay.

Anthony Okolie: Alright. Let's talk about tariffs and uncertainty. We've heard a lot about the tariffs-- US tariff policy that's caused a lot of geopolitical trade uncertainty. How have those tensions, particularly between the US and China, how is that impacting commodities?

Tim Yang: So US-China competition, everybody realizes this is real. The superpower competition is here to stay. So the first competition they will have is technological competitions-- collect data, building data centers. So everybody knows this.

And then the second thing is, particularly for the United States, they realize there is a need to secure supply chain for critical minerals. If you see President Trump going to meet all the foreign leaders, critical minerals is a topic always come up.

The third thing that I want to mention is, it's not just about mining minerals, it's actually processing them. It's even more challenging. For example, rare earths. So, rare earth is more earth than rare. It's not rare to find the rare earth. But the problem, is we need to process them. We need literally to crush them, grind it up, and chemically separate them, and then refine them and then reconstitute to make it to a more useful form, to make magnet of it. So this process is 9 to 13 times more energy-intensive than just get out of the ground. We need a lot of energy. We need a lot of water. We need the transportation infrastructure. There's a massive need for this theme as well.

Anthony Okolie: Alright. And let's talk about these commodities with regards to supply side. We've heard a lot about the underinvestment in the commodity sector for some time. What do you see there?

Tim Yang: We've talked about demand-- the supply side is equally important. So what we've seen over the last 20 years, there was underinvestment on the supply side. It's particularly important now when the inventory is tight, it becomes a problem. So the mine quality, the mine grade has been deteriorating, and the mining activities become dangerous.

We've seen accidents happen in Indonesia, in DRC. We've seen earthquake that happened in Chile. So a lot of mining supply disruptions over the years. This is the first thing I want to mention.

And the second thing is, for a lot of countries who have minerals, they realize it's a national security to have control over those minerals. For example, Guinea has a lot of bauxite. Throughout the year, there was export control of the bauxite that we needed to make aluminum. That's a problem.

And for [the] Indonesian government, they have asked for control for nickel ores. That's a problem. So, we needed to address that issue as well and make investment also help with local communities.

We're not sure, when there was a surge in demand, with all the themes we just mentioned, is supply able to keep up?

Original Post

data.title: ASG: Liberty All-Star Growth Offers A 9.3% Yield, But Performance Lags
data.description:
Liberty All-Star Growth (ASG) is a closed end fund that invests in growth, period. It has three managers, each bringing with them expertise in a one of the three market capitalization categories - small, medium, and large.

ALPS Advisors, Inc. oversees and keeps an eye on the performance of the managers. This is to " ensure that they are performing as expected and adhering to their style and strategy, and will replace the managers when warranted ". This is not just lip service. In a July 2024 interview, Robert Milas, the Director of Research at AALP Advisors noted that there had been 10 manager changes since they took over the reins of ASG in 1995. In case it is still unclear, ASG is an actively managed fund.

Holdings

Currently ASG has around $380 million in assets under management.

Essentially all of the funds are invested in equities as we can see above. At October 31, Information Technology formed about a third of the portfolio, followed by Industrials, Health Care and Consumer Discretionary, with the latter barely making it to the double digit category.

The fund also had a portion of the equity portfolio invested in Financials, Communication Services, Real Estate and Consumer Staples. Individually, six of the Magnificent Seven occupied the first six spots in the Top 10 Holdings list.

Tesla, Inc (TSLA) is noticeably missing, but fret not, it was part of the portfolio, albeit just a bit further down the totem pole at 0.47%. We have penned our thoughts on few of the above noted technology darlings in the past, namely NVIDIA Corp (NVDA), Apple Inc (AAPL), Amazon.com Inc (AMZN) and TSLA.

Expenses

ASG's annual expense ratio is 1.18%, two thirds of which is for Management and Advisory fees.

This expense ratio is typical for closed end funds that use little to no leverage, like our protagonist, ASG.

Distribution

ASG pays distributions on a quarterly basis and the amount is variable.

The method applied to determine the distribution amount is interesting.

The current policy is to pay distributions on its shares totaling approximately 8 percent of its net asset value per year, payable in four quarterly installments of 2 percent of the Fund’s net asset value at the close of the New York Stock Exchange on the Friday prior to each quarterly declaration date.

Source: 2024 Annual Report

Applying the latest distribution amount to the current market price of $5.15, ASG has an annualized yield of 9.3%.

Performance

ASG's performance is benchmarked to the Lipper Multi-Cap Growth Mutual Fund Average. Besides, its aforementioned primary benchmark, the 2024 Annual Report (year ending December 31) also shows ASG's performance in comparison to the Dow Jones Industrial Average, the NASDAQ Composite Index, the Russell Growth Average and the S&P 500 Index.

In absolute terms, 2024 was a good year for the ASG investors, but only if they did not compare their returns with those of the primary and secondary benchmarks noted above. The reason for the lag is unsurprising:

With large- and mega-cap growth stock returns surpassing those of small- and mid-cap growth stocks by a significant margin, it was difficult for our diversified growth fund with exposure across the market capitalization range to keep pace in 2024.... A narrow band of large- and mega-cap growth stocks performed exceptionally well—so well that they pulled many key market measures along with them.

Source: 2024 Annual Report

ASG is diversified across market capitalizations, so it will be hard for the fund or any diversified portfolio to beat a market powered by less than a hand full of companies.

The S&P 500’s performance remained highly concentrated throughout the year, with only 28 percent of stocks in the index exceeding the index’s return. The Magnificent Seven alone accounted for 53 percent of the index’s return (and 62 percent in 2023), further demonstrating the impact of a concentrated group of stocks on the return of the overall index

Source: 2024 Annual Report

Over the longer term, ASG has lagged its primary benchmark. It has trailed even more notably over the last three and five years.

The year to date numbers are the most atypical and lag by a huge margin. The market has not liked this one bit and that is reflected in the market price return relative to the net asset value return. While this fund is no stranger to trading at a discount to its net asset value, the current discount is as steep as it has been in the last five years.

This is evident in the premium/discount summary and the Z-Score data provided below.

Outlook & Verdict

ASG has struggled to deliver in a growth utopia. There are a few different reasons for this. The first being that passive flows have been the real big story. Outperforming an index with no fees has always been hard, outperforming it in an era of passive flows has been next to impossible. Valuations have mattered little to growth investors and any active management trying to look for relative value has been a flop.

The fund also has a handicapping distribution policy. Growth stocks by default pay very little in the form of dividends. So when the fund tries to combine growth stocks with an 8% annual NAV distribution, you are asking (no, begging), for trouble. To generate said yield, you have to sell stocks every quarter. You hence sell more stock when the market is down and less when it is up. It works out like buy high, sell low.

Finally, the fund has been awful in bear markets. Full cycle returns rely on capital preservation and you have to save capital in downturns. The fund NAV lost 2X as much as the category during the last bear market of 2022.

Overall, this is an ok fund if you are trying to straddle the different market capitalizations and have growth exposure. For us, growth valuations look atrocious (99th percentile) in the large cap space and fairly high (80th percentile) in mid cap space. Small caps area does offer some choices but we are not sure we would want this fund to go hunting for us there either. The one real plus here is the Z-score and what that means if you ever wanted to go long the fund, this is not a bad time to do so. We rate this a "hold".

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: U.K. CPI: Soft October Inflation Strengthens The Case For A December BoE Cut
data.description:
UK CPI inflation came in broadly as expected in October, rising 0.4% MoM and 3.6% YoY. Both of these rates were in line with analyst expectations. Importantly, the annual rate saw its first deceleration since May 2025, falling from the 3.8% YoY readings in the previous three months to the 3.6% YoY rate in October. The report today is largely being interpreted as a signal that inflation has peaked and will continue to decline going into the first half of 2026.

Housing and Household Services

The deceleration in the annual rate came almost entirely from one component, housing & household services, which is affected by the volatility in energy prices. The segment’s annual inflation rate slowed to 5.0% YoY in October from 5.9% YoY in September (the lowest since September 2024), driven largely by a sharp cooling in gas and electricity costs following the latest Ofgem price cap adjustment. Gas prices rose just 2.1% YoY after a 13.0% YoY jump the month before, and electricity saw a similar downshift to 2.7% YoY from 8.0% YoY. The segment was also impacted by lower inflation in housing. Owner-occupiers’ housing costs continued to ease, rising 4.8% YoY in October (compared with 5.2% YoY in September), the ninth straight slowdown and the lowest since August 2023.

These softer annual comparisons, combined with far milder monthly increases than last year’s double-digit surges, helped pull the broader housing & household services index lower and reinforced the view that energy volatility remains a key swing factor for services inflation. Base effects played a role, as housing & household services rose only 0.7% MoM in October 2025 versus a much larger 2.7% MoM increase in October 2024. Together, the developments in both the energy and non-energy housing & household services subsegments provided much of the downside impulse to October’s headline inflation rate, specifically causing the annual CPI rate to fall by -0.25 ppts compared with September.

Food

One component that was a bit hot was food and non-alcoholic beverages, and notably, its contribution saw the largest increase from September to October (+0.04 ppts). The upward pressure came almost entirely from food, which makes up 90% of the component and posted a firm 0.5% MoM gain in October, well above the 0.1% MoM rise a year earlier. Several major categories accelerated meaningfully: bread and cereals were up 1.2% MoM versus flat a year ago, meat rose 0.4% MoM versus flat a year ago, and vegetables increased 0.8% MoM compared with 0.2% MoM a year ago. Smaller categories also swung higher, with fish, sugar and confectionery, and oils and fats all rising in October 2025 after declines in October 2024. The overall picture showed broad-based momentum across processed food and non-alcoholic beverages (+0.5% MoM), non-processed foods (+0.5% MoM), and seasonal foods (+0.6% MoM). On an annual basis, the component reversed most of its prior dip, rising back to 4.9% YoY in October after falling to 4.5% YoY in September.

Core CPI

The other components outside of housing & household services and food & non-alcoholic saw mostly benign movement that had minimal impact on the trend in the annual rate. And more importantly, the general trend across prices is one of disinflation. This is demonstrated by the continuation across the broader CPI aggregates: goods, services, and core:

  • Goods prices increased 0.5% MoM in October 2025, weaker than 0.8% MoM in October 2024, and the annual rate slowed from 2.9% YoY in September to 2.6% YoY in October.
  • Services prices increased 0.2% MoM in October 2025, weaker than 0.4% MoM in October 2024, and the annual rate slowed from 4.7% YoY in September to 4.5% YoY in October.
  • Core CPI (which excludes food, energy, alcohol, and tobacco) increased 0.3% MoM in October 2025, weaker than 0.4% MoM in October 2024, and the annual rate slowed from 3.5% YoY in September to 3.4% YoY in October. It is also worth noting that the monthly rate was slightly below the consensus estimate of 0.4% MoM.

So yes, the disinflation trend is gradual, but it is still there, and more importantly, it developed as expected with substantial movement in the energy-related indexes.

Why is this important? The Bank of England (BOE) explained in its November monetary policy announcement that “further reductions will therefore depend on the evolution of the outlook for inflation.” Specifically, the BoE wants to continue to see a healthy trend of disinflation develop such that CPI inflation slows to 3.2% by March 2026 (a view explicitly outlined in the November Monetary Policy Report). This scenario is based on the contribution from energy falling to just 0.2-0.3 ppts by Q1 2026 and services inflation slowing from 4.7% YoY in September 2025 to 4.3% YoY in March 2026. The October report showed solid progress in both of these specific areas and, in my view, puts the BoE on track to cut rates in the December meeting.

Market Reaction

Financial markets did not trade the cooling in UK inflation in a clear manner. In fact, the moves in equities and rates were volatile over the course of the trading day, even though the market saw a cut in December as slightly more likely (86% chance of a cut in December today, up from an 80% chance priced on Tuesday, per Reuters).

  • The UK FTSE 100 Index finished the day essentially flat, drifting higher and lower throughout the session with no sustained direction in response to the inflation data. Even so, trading showed a mildly dovish tilt, as UK large caps lagged more rate-sensitive parts of the market. The FTSE 250 Index rose 0.4%, and the FTSE SmallCap Index edged up 0.1%, both outperforming the FTSE 100 and hinting at a modest preference for domestically focused, interest-rate-exposed equities.
  • The movement in rates was especially volatile today across all durations. Gilt rates moved lower following the cool CPI data but then moved sharply in both directions around 6 am EST before eventually drifting higher towards the end of the UK session. In the end, short-term rates were lower to flat (6-month down -5 bps, 2-year unchanged), more reflective of higher rate cut expectations, and longer-term rates increased (5-year up 2 bps, 10-year up 4 bps), possibly in response to expectations that the UK Fall budget next week might come in more expansionary than expected.
  • Sterling’s reaction was mixed after an initially dovish move. GBPUSD was down about -0.3% by the end of the UK session, while GBPEUR recovered to flat after earlier losses, and GBPJPY strengthened 0.4% after a muted start. These intraday swings closely tracked the volatility in UK rate markets, which ultimately muddied what might have otherwise been a more straightforwardly dovish response to the softer inflation data.

The October UK inflation report was shaped largely by a pronounced disinflationary pull from the energy component, while most other categories made only marginal contributions to the shift in the annual rate. The food component is worth watching, as it came in a bit hotter than expected, though the pickup may simply reflect normal monthly volatility in September and October before the broader disinflation trend resumes. With this being the only CPI release the Bank of England will see ahead of its December meeting, the softer print gives policymakers some room to consider a cut after November’s pause, particularly in light of the sharp rise in the September unemployment rate. A move in that direction could offer some relief for UK equities, which have struggled through a difficult November.

data.title: Asbury Automotive Group: Attractively Valued Amid A Challenging Economic Backdrop
data.description:
Introduction

2025 has proven to be a turbulent year for the U.S. economy, characterized by elevated uncertainty, job market weakness, and lack of up-to-date information stemming from the longest government shutdown in U.S. history.

Against this background, consumer discretionary stocks such as Asbury Automotive Group (ABG) have had a challenging year, as evidenced by the weak overall performance for the Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLY), which is up only 1% year-to-date:

In this difficult environment, ABG reported solid Q3 2025 results, enjoying a mix of organic and inorganic growth. As such, I confirm my Buy rating on ABG, driven by:

  • A very undemanding adjusted P/E multiple of 7.8x relative to 2025-2026 expectations, lower than when I last covered the company in 2024.
  • Expected acceleration in U.S. economic momentum in 2026, with GDP growth reaching 2.1%.
  • Attractive enterprise-level valuation and a well-laddered debt maturity profile.

In this article, I will expand on these points and discuss risks related to the investment case.

Company Overview

You can access all company results here. Asbury Automotive Group is an auto retailer operating in two segments - Dealerships and Total Care Auto, or TCA. While new and used cars account for respectively 52% and 30% of the company's 9M 2025 revenue, the main profit drivers are Parts and Service (48% of 9M 2025 gross profit) and Finance and Insurance, or F&I, at 23% of the company's 9M 2025 gross profit:

ABG's main geographic focus is on the Sun Belt and Mountain West, although the highest store count is in Massachusetts (31 stores) after the Herb Chambers acquisition, followed by Florida (24) and Colorado (17), as shown in the store map below:

I estimate that the company's enterprise value totals $7.75 billion, of which 55% is funded with common equity, leaving 45% for net debt. The company does not pay a dividend, relying on retained earnings to reduce debt after each acquisition.

Q3 2025 Results Overview

ABG reported adjusted EPS of $7.17/share, up 13% Y/Y. The principal driver was continued portfolio growth, with revenue and gross profit increasing 13% and 12% respectively year-over-year.

To account for changes in store counts (which are the result and acquisitions/disposals), ABG also reports same-store metrics. There the company reported revenue growth of 5% Y/Y, although the gross profit increase was only 3% Y/Y. This was due to stronger revenue growth in low-margin new vehicle sales:

Overall we observe that ABG enjoys a healthy mix of organic and inorganic growth fractionally above the circa 3% inflation rate.

2026 Economic Outlook

I believe this financial performance is reasonable when we factor in ongoing uncertainty stemming from a new U.S. administration under President Trump. While any potential disruption to sales resulting from the longest lasting U.S. government shutdown will only show up in Q4 2025 results, with the shutdown over, attention now turns to the 2026 economic outlook.

After a sharp slowdown in 2025 (the IMF expects GDP growth of just 2% in the United States this year, down 0.8% Y/Y), the outlook for 2026 is marginally better, with GDP seen expanding at 2.1% Y/Y:

An accelerating GDP growth should be favorable for consumer discretionary companies such as ABG. What is more, ongoing Fed rate cuts should result in a Fed funds rate close to 3% by the end of 2026 (judging by Fed funds futures prices). This will have a favorable impact on financing costs for vehicle sales.

While a sizable 45% of enterprise value is funded with net debt, ABG's well-staggered maturity structure will result in limited benefits from refinancing 2026 liabilities:

ABG Valuation

The company reported 9M 2025 adjusted EPS of $21.42/share, with analysts expecting full-year earnings of $28.26/share, or EPS of only $6.84/share in Q4 2025:

While I agree that some weakness stemming from the government shutdown is likely in Q4 2025, ongoing share repurchases ($50 million in Q3 2025) in my opinion put risks tilted to the upside with regards to Q4 2025 earnings.

2026 EPS estimates seem even more pessimistic, at just $28.31/share, essentially flat year-over-year. Indeed even if we disregard share repurchases (which at the current pace would lift EPS roughly 4.5%), the outlook for accelerating GDP growth and lower interest rates should, all else equal, translate into better earnings at ABG.

Against these expectations, ABG trades at only 7.8x its 2025-2026 adjusted EPS estimates, a real bargain as long as GDP growth lives up to expectations.

Since the company uses 45% of debt in its capital structure, it makes sense to look at enterprise-level valuation as well. ABG has 19.6 million in diluted shares outstanding, indicating adjusted earnings of roughly $555 million in 2026. Interest expenses outside of floor plan financing stood at $51.3 million in Q3 2025, indicating an annual run-rate close to $205 million.

Hence we arrive at cumulative enterprise-level cash flows of around $760 million in 2026, or a 9.8% yield relative to the $7.75 billion enterprise value. For a business growing in line with/marginally above inflation, I think this is an attractive investment opportunity in itself.

Overall I still see ABG as attractively valued going into 2026, confirming my previous Buy rating on the shares.

Risks

As with any consumer discretionary stock, the principal risk to consider is a potential U.S. recession. I believe recession risks are currently above average, stemming from a weak labor market. Still, as long as the economy manages to overcome near-term weakness, the outlook for 2026 should be better, as shown in the IMF outlook.

Another macro-related risk is that the Fed may take a more cautious approach towards policy normalization. This will delay any potential benefits from lower financing costs.

Turning to ABG-specific risks, the company's inorganic growth strategy has served it well in recent years, with revenue growth of 19% annually since 2019:

That said, execution risk always remains a concern when buying competitors. As such, there is no guarantee that ABG will continue its successful acquisition streak, which has allowed it to grow adjusted EPS by 24% annually since 2019.

Conclusion

After a rough 2025 for the U.S. economy investors in consumer discretionary companies such as ABG hope to breathe a sigh of relief in 2026. Indeed accelerating GDP growth and lower interest rates should provide a strong tailwind to already attractive valuations.

ABG trades at just 7.8x its 2025-2026 earnings, with analysts pricing in essentially no EPS growth next year. Amid ongoing share repurchases and an ever-growing portfolio, this remains a conservative assumption.

The company uses a well-laddered debt structure, indicating little immediate benefit from lower interest rates on financing costs. Even so, I estimate 2026 enterprise-level cash flows at about $760 million, indicating a return of 9.8% relative to the $7.75 billion enterprise value.

I believe this remains attractive against the favorable U.S. economic outlook in 2026, confirming my previous Buy rating on the shares.

Key risks in the investment case include a U.S. recession, slowed Fed rate cuts, and execution risks for new acquisitions.

Thank you for reading.
